IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST REPUBLIC
OF SRI LANKA
Assistant Commissioner of Co-operative Development, (Kandy District), Department of Central Province Co-operative Development, Ehelepola Kumarihamy Road, Bogambara, Kandy.

Vs Assistant Commissioner of Co-operative Development, (Kandy District), Department of Central Province Co-operative Development, Ehelepola Kumarihamy Road, Bogambara, Kandy.

Depositor-Respondent-Respondent Before : D. THOTAWATTA, J. K. M. S. DISSANAYAKE, J. Counsel : Priyantha Alagiyawanna with Heshani
Gunaratne instructed by Bushra Hashim
for the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant and
2nd to 4th Petitioner-Appellants.

Written Submissions of the Defendant-Petitioner -Appellant and 2nd to 4th Petitioner-Appellants tendered on : 14.11.2022 and 10.03.2026 Written Submissions of the Plaintiff-Respondent -Respondent tendered on : Not tendered Written Submissions of the 1st Respondent- Respondent,
Page 5 of 17
the 2nd Respondent- Respondent and the Depositor Respondent- Respondent tendered on : Not tendered Decided on : 10.07.2026
K. M. S. DISSANAYAKE, J.
The Accused/Defendant-Petitioner and the 2nd to 4th Petitioners mentioned in
the notice had in the application in revision bearing No. ප්‍රති 132/18 filed by them
in the High Court of the Central Province holden at Kandy (hereinafter called and
referred to as ‘the High Court of the Province’) invoked the extra-ordinary
revisionary jurisdiction of the High Court of the Province against an order made
by the learned Additional Magistrate of Kandy, dated 23.10.2018, (hereinafter
called and referred to as “the MC order”) over-ruling the preliminary legal
objection raised by the 2nd to 4th Petitioners mentioned in the notice with regard
to the application made thereto by the Assistant Commissioner of Co-operative
Development-the Plaintiff-Respondent thereto, seeking to add the 2nd to 4th
Petitioners mentioned in the notice as parties to the action instituted thereat, by
it upon furnishing a certificate thereto, under and in terms of section 59(4) of
the Co-operative Society Statute of the Central Province of 10 of 1990- published
in the Government Gazette bearing No. 75 and dated 22.01.1993 (hereinafter
called and referred to as “the Statute”) and the learned High Court Judge of the
Central Province holden at Kandy (hereinafter called and referred to as “the
learned High Court Judge of the Province”) had in the order dated 12.12.2018
(hereinafter called and referred to as ‘the HC order’), dismissed it in-limine by
holding that Rule 26 of the Co-operative Societies Rules of 1973 (hereinafter
called and referred to as ‘the Co-operative Societies Rules’) permits the Directors
of a Co-operative Society to be added as a party to an action instituted by the
Commissioner of Co-operative Development under and in terms of section 59(4)
of the Statute in instances where circumstances stipulated in Rule 26 of the Co-
operative Societies Rules do warrant such a cause. It is this order that the
Page 6 of 17
Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant and the noticed received 2nd to 4th Petitioner-
Appellants (hereinafter called and referred to as “the Appellants”) now, seek to
canvass before this Court in the instant appeal.
It is to be noted at this juncture, that when the instant appeal bearing No.
CA(PHC)0217/2018 came on before us for argument on 26.01.2026, the learned
Counsel for the Appellants as well as the learned State Counsel for the Plaintiff-
Respondent-Respondent (hereinafter called and referred to as ‘the Plaintiff-
Respondent’) in Appeals bearing Nos. CA(PHC)0218/2018 and
CA(PHC)0219/2018 informed Court that the parties thereto, too, are agreeing
to be bound by the judgement of this Court in the present appeal bearing No.
CA(PHC)0217/2018 and hence, the judgement in the instant appeal bearing
No. CA(PHC)0217/2018 will bind all the parties in Appeals bearing Nos.
CA(PHC)0218/2018 and CA(PHC)0219/2018 too, as urged and agreed by the
respective Counsel.
Furthermore, respective Counsel for the parties had informed Court that instant
appeal can be disposed of by way of written submissions and hence, the parties
were directed to furnish to Court the written submissions within the period of
time stipulated by Court. However, it is to be observed that written submissions
for the Plaintiff-Respondent had not been furnished to this Court despite a
significant number of dates were allowed by this Court for that purpose as urged
by the learned State Counsel. Hence, the judgement will be pronounced on the
strength of the material available on record and the written submissions so
furnished to Court by the Appellants as urged and agreed.
Let me now, briefly, set out as follows the facts material and relevant to the
instant appeal as is discernible from the petition of appeal.
The 1st Appellant being a registered Co-operative society which can sue and be
sued by its corporate name; and that the 2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants are the
Directors of the 1st Appellant Co-operative Society; and that the Dispositor-
Respondent had by way of letter of demand, demanded from the 1st Appellant
Page 7 of 17
the monies deposited thereat, by him being a member thereof; and that since he
was not a member of the 1st Appellant Co-operative Society and since the
payment if any, should be paid after the recovery of the loans given by it, the 1st
Appellant was not in a position to pay the said sums of monies to him; and that
in the circumstances the Plaintiff-Respondent had referred the dispute between
the 1st Appellant-Co-operative Society and the Depositor-Respondent to the
Arbitrator for arbitration; and that the Arbitrator had having arbitrated the
dispute between the aforesaid parties, awarded an arbitral award in favour of
the Depositor-Respondent and against the 1st Appellant-Co-operative Society;
and that the Assistant Commissioner of Co-operative Development-the Plaintiff-
Respondent had upon the 1st Appellant’s failure to honour the arbitral award,
issued a certificate to the Magistrate Court of Kandy for the enforcement of it by
virtue of the powers vested in it by section 59(4) of the Statute in an action
bearing No. 9550/18 naming therein, the 1st Appellant as the defaulter within
the meaning of section 59(4) of the Statute; and that pending the final
determination of the matter, the Assistant Commissioner of Co-operative
Development-the Plaintiff-Respondent had by way of a motion, furnished to the
Magistrate Court a list setting out the names of the Board of Directors of the 1st
Appellant-Co-operative Society to which the 2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants who
appeared in Court upon notice, had objected and the learned Additional
Magistrate of Kandy had having inquired thereto, made the impugned order (the
MC order) allowing the application of the Assistant Commissioner of Co-operative
Development-the Plaintiff-Respondent thereby, permitting it to add 2nd to 4th
Appellants as parties thereto, and proceed accordingly, against which
application in revision had been filed in the High Court of the Province seeking
to revise and set aside the MC order which too, was dismissed by it and it is
against this Order that the instant appeal has now, been preferred to this Court
by the Appellants.
The Appellants in the application in revision preferred to the High Court of the
Province, had invited it to invoke the extra-ordinary revisionary jurisdiction
Page 8 of 17
vested in it by Article 154P(3)(b) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist
Republic of Sri Lanka to revise and set aside the MC order. It is in this context,
I would think it expedient at this juncture to examine the law governing the
exercise of the extra-ordinary revisionary jurisdiction vested in an Appellate
Court.
After an exhaustive analysis of all the authorities in relation to the exercise of
the extra-ordinary revisionary jurisdiction, it was held in Rustom vs.
Hapangama & Company (1978-79) 2 SLR 225, that “the powers by way of
revision conferred on the Appellate Court are wide and can be exercised whether
an appeal has been taken against an order of the original Court or not. However,
such powers would be exercised only in exceptional circumstances where
an appeal lay and as to what such exceptional circumstances are dependent
on the facts of each case”. [emphasis is mine]
Court in Rustom vs. Hapangama & Co (Supra) further observed in the following
manner; “The trend of authority clearly, indicates that where the revisionary
powers of the Court of Appeal are invoked, the practice has been that these
powers will be exercised if there is an alternative remedy available, only if the
existence of special circumstances are urged necessitating the indulgence of this
Court to exercise its powers in revision.”
It was further observed by Court in the decision in Rustom Vs. Hapangama &
Company(Supra) that, “This Court has the power to act in revision even though
the appeal is available, in appropriate cases. The question which has now to be
decided is whether the instant case is an appropriate case in which we should
exercise our discretionary powers of revision. In his petition and affidavit the
petitioner has not set out the reasons for his seeking this method of rectification
of the order rather than the ordinary method of appeal. Nor has he set out any
exceptional circumstances as to why we should grant him the indulgence of
exercising our revisionary powers when he could have appealed against the order
with leave”.
Page 9 of 17
It was held by Court in Dharmaratne and Another Vs. Palm Paradise Cabanas
Ltd and Others 2003 (3) SLR 24 at page 30 that “Thus, the existence of
exceptional circumstances is the process by which the Court selects the cases in
respect of which this extra-ordinary method of rectification should be adopted.
If such a selection process is not there, revisionary jurisdiction of this Court will
become a gateway for every litigant to make a second appeal in the grab of a
revision application or to make an appeal in situations where the legislature has
not given right of appeal. The practice of Court to insist on the existence of
exceptional circumstances for the exercise of revisionary powers has taken deep
root in our law and has got hardened into a rule which should not be lightly
disturbed. The words used by the legislature do not indicate that it ever intended
to interfere with this ‘rule of practice’.”
It was held in Urban Development Authority vs. Ceylon Entertainments Ltd.
and another reported in 2002 [B.L.R] at page 66 that “the petitioner who
invokes the revisionary jurisdiction of this Court should expressly set out the
exceptional circumstances and an express pleading to that effect is a sine quo
non, for this Court to consider the relief claimed by the petitioner. In the absence
of an express plea, this Court would be precluded from considering the relief
claimed in the application”.
Let me now, quote a passage from the decision in Perera v. Muthalib 45 NLR
412 to the following effect;
“I would invite attention to the observations made by Wood-Renton J. in
the King v. Nordeen. He said; I do not think that that power (i.e. ,
revisionary power) is at all limited to those cases in which either no appeal
lies or for some reason or other an appeal has not been taken”, but he
went on to add that this power would be exercised only when a strong case
is made out “amounting to a positive miscarriage of justice in regard to
either the law, or the judge’s appreciation of the facts. In the case I am
dealing with I should have felt compelled to give relief solely on the ground
Page 10 of 17
that what may well be said to be a failure of justice has been brought to
the notice of this Court, and technical rules must make way for the
granting of redress in such a case. There has been a violation of the
fundamental rule of judicial procedure that a person sought to be affected
by an order shall first be heard. But, in this instance there is yet another
ground upon which this application for revision ought to be exercised and
that is that the petitioner had no knowledge of the order made against him
till the time for preferring an appeal had elapsed.”
In the case of Attorney-General v. Podi Singho 51 NLR 385 it was held that
“even though the revisionary powers should not be exercised in cases when there
is an appeal and was not taken, the revisionary powers should be exercised only
in exceptional circumstances such as (a) miscarriage of justice (b) where a strong
case for interference by the Supreme Court is made out or (c) where the applicant
was unaware of the order.” Court also observes that “the Supreme Court in
exercising its powers of revision is not hampered by technical rules of pleading
and procedure.”
In Bank of Ceylon vs Kaleel and others 2004 [1] SLR 284, at page 287 it was
stated that, “In the circumstances this Court will not interfere by way of revision
when the law has given the plaintiff an alternate remedy and when the plaintiff
has not shown the existence of exceptional circumstances warranting the
exercise of revisionary jurisdiction. In any event, for this Court to exercise
revisionary jurisdiction the order challenged must have occasioned a failure of
justice and be manifestly erroneous which go beyond an error or defect or
irregularity that an ordinary person would instantly react to it. In other words
the order complained of is of such a nature which would have shocked the
conscience of Court.”
In Thilagaratnam Vs. E.A.P. Edirisingha 1982 [1] SLR, P56, it was observed
that “though the Appellate Courts' powers to act in revision were wide and would
be exercised whether an appeal has been taken against the order of the original
Page 11 of 17
court or not, such powers would be exercised only in exceptional circumstances.
There were no exceptional circumstances in this case to justify the exercise of
the Court's powers of revision.”
In Cadaman Pulle Vs. Ceylon Paper Sacks Ltd 2001 [3] SLR, P112, it was held
that “no exceptional circumstances are disclosed why his application for
revisionary relief should be entertained after the lapse of nearly two years. The
existence of exceptional circumstances is a pre-condition for the exercise of the
powers of revision.”
The Supreme Court, in the case of Rasheed Ali Vs. Mohamed Ali 1981 [1] SLR,
P262, held that, “the powers of revision vested in the Court of Appeal are very
wide and the Court can in a fit case exercise that power whether or not an appeal
lies. Where the law does not give a right of appeal and makes the order final, the
Court of Appeal may nevertheless exercise its powers of revision, but it should
do so only in exceptional circumstances. Ordinarily, the Court will not interfere
by way of review, particularly when the law has expressly given an aggrieved
party an alternate remedy such as the right to file a separate action except when
non-interference will cause a denial of justice or irremediable
harm…………………… The fact that a Judge's order is merely wrong is not a
sufficient ground for exercising the powers of revision.”
The Supreme Court in AG. Vs. Najimudeen-SC Appeal No. 62/2016-SC Minute
of 13-03-2020 observed that, “the Defendant has had an alternative remedy
available. In the instant case, what the Provincial High Court was called upon to
exercise was its revisionary jurisdiction. The Defendant has not been successful
in convincing Court that the grounds he had urged have any exceptional
character which is sufficient to move Court to exercise its discretionary
revisionary power. Thus, this Court has no reason to disagree with the
conclusion of the Provincial High Court that there are no exceptional
circumstances to invoke the revisionary jurisdiction of the Court. In these
Page 12 of 17
circumstances and for the foregoing reasons, the appeal is dismissed without
costs.”
In Geethani Nilushika Vs. Waruni Harshika-SC Appeal No. 93/2017-SC
Minute of 18.06.2021 Court observed that; “Revision is a discretionary
remedy. A party cannot invoke this extraordinary jurisdiction of the
Appellate Court as of right. When a right of appeal is available against a
Judgment or an Order, a party seeking to come before Court by way of
revision shall explain in the petition why he did not exercise his right of
appeal. In the revision application filed before the High Court there was no
such explanation at all.”
In the case of Seylan bank Vs. Thangaveil 2004 [2] SLR, 101 wherein Court
held that, “no revision lies. The petitioner has not resorted to his statutory right
of appeal with leave of court. He has not set out in his petition for revision any
exceptional circumstances.”
See also; Hotel Galaxy (Pvt) Ltd Vs. Mercantile Hotels Management Ltd.
1978 (1) SLR 05, Janita Vs. Abeysekara (Sri skantha Law Report Vol iv Page
22), and Thilagaratnam Vs. Edirisinghe 1982 (1) SLR 56.
In the light of the principle laid down by Court in the aforesaid decisions, an
Appellate Court can exercise its revisionary jurisdiction conferred on the
Appellate Court only and only, in exceptional circumstances, where an
alternative remedy lay. [emphasis is mine]
Having borne in mind the law set out therein, let me now, deal with the instant
appeal.
Procedure to be adopted by a Magistrate upon a receipt of a certificate under
section 59(4) of the Statute has been laid down by the very same section in that
the learned Magistrate upon receipt of a certificate thereunder, make order
issuing notices on the defaulter named therein, to show cause why further
proceedings should not be taken against him for the recovery of the sums of
Page 13 of 17
monies stated in the certificate; and that in case, the defaulter does not show
sufficient cause therefor, the sums of monies mentioned in the certificate should
be recovered as a fine imposed by a Magistrate to which the provisions in section
291 of the Criminal Procedure Code would be applicable except for provision
contained in 291(1)(c) thereof.
A careful scrutiny of section 59(4) of the Statute becomes abundantly, clear that
it would afford an opportunity to the defaulter by way of show cause, to show
that he is not indeed, the defaulter of the sums of monies mentioned in the
certificates issued to the Magistrate Court thereunder.
Hence, section 59(4) of the Statute provides an effective, efficacious and
expeditious remedy for a defaulter by way of show cause, to show that he is not
indeed, the defaulter of the sums of monies mentioned in the certificates issued
to the Magistrate Court thereunder in the proceedings before the Magistrate.
In the light of the law set out above, when the learned Additional Magistrate of
Kandy in the order impugned (the MC order), permitted the Assistant
Commissioner of Co-operative Development-Plaintiff-Respondent to name the
2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants too, as defaulter of the sums of monies mentioned in
the certificate filed before it under section 59(4) of the Statute thereby, affording
the 2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants too, an opportunity to show cause why further
proceedings should not be taken against them for the recovery of the sums of
monies stated in the certificate filed before the Magistrate of Kandy as required
by section 59(4) of the Statute wherein, they would have shown cause that they
are not indeed, the defaulter of the sums of monies mentioned, had they availed
themselves of the remedy so provided for by section 59(4) of the Statute.
However, it is significant to observe that the 2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants had not
in any manner, opted to resort to the effective, efficacious and expeditious
remedy so provided for by section 59(4) of the Statute for a defaulter by way of
show cause, to show that they are not indeed, the defaulter of the sums of monies
mentioned in the certificates issued to the Magistrate Court thereunder in the
Page 14 of 17
proceedings before the Magistrate, instead, they had not only, opted to object to
the application made to the Magistrate Court of Kandy by the Assistant
Commissioner of Co-operative Development-the Plaintiff-Respondent for them to
be added as the defaulter of the sums of monies mentioned in the certificate
issued to the Magistrate Court of Kandy by the Assistant Commissioner of Co-
operative Development-the Plaintiff-Respondent but also, had they opted to
institute an application in revision inviting the High Court of the Province to
revise and set aside the order of the learned Magistrate permitting the application
to add 2nd to 4th Appellants as parties thereto, and proceed accordingly. They
had not stopped at that, but that they had opted to prefer the instant appeal to
this Court from the dismissal by the High Court of the province of the application
in revision preferred thereto, by the 2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants from the MC order.
However, a careful perusal of the record manifestly, shows that not an iota of
evidence had been adduced by the 2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants firstly, in the
petition filed along with the application in revision in the High Court of the
Province or else in the petition of appeal filed to this Court why they did not avail
themselves of the effective, efficacious and expeditious remedy so provided for by
section 59(4) of the Statute for a defaulter by way of show cause, to show that
he is not indeed, the defaulter of the sums of monies mentioned in the certificates
issued to the Magistrate Court thereunder in the proceedings before the
Magistrate, before they had opted to move in revision and thereafter to file the
instant appeal to this Court despite the effective, efficacious and expeditious
remedy so provided for by section 59(4) of the Statute for a defaulter by way of
show cause, to show that he is not indeed, the defaulter of the sums of monies
mentioned in the certificates issued to the Magistrate Court thereunder in the
proceedings before the Magistrate.
Hence, I am of the view that the application in revision preferred by the
Appellants to the High Court of the Province ought to have been dismissed in-
limine on this ground alone in view of their total failure to avail themselves of the
effective, efficacious and expeditious remedy so provided for by section 59(4) of
Page 15 of 17
the Statute for a defaulter by way of show cause, to show that they are not
indeed, the defaulter of the sums of monies mentioned in the certificates issued
to the Magistrate Court thereunder in the proceedings before the Magistrate
upon them being named as defaulter by the learned Additional Magistrate of
Kandy in the order impugned (the MC order), though not directed the judicial
mind of the learned High Court Judge of the Province to this pivotal and pertinent
question that ought to have been taken into consideration by Court in
determining the availability of this extra-ordinary method of rectification in
revision of the MC order on the alleged grounds disclosed in the petition filed in
the High Court of the Province by the Appellants.
Let me now, examine the reasons adduced by the learned Additional Magistrate
of Kandy for overruling the preliminary legal objection so raised by the Appellants
to the application to add the 2nd, 3rd and 4th Appellants too, as defaulter to the
sums of monies mentioned in the certificate filed in the Magistrate Court of
Kandy under 59(4) of the Statute by the Assistant Commissioner of Co-operative
Development-Plaintiff-Respondent and the reasons adduced therefor, being that
Rule 72 of the Co-operative Societies Rules permits Board of Directors of a Co-
operative Society too, to be named as a defaulter in a certificate filed by the
Commissioner of Co-operative Development under section 59(4) of the Statute
in instances set out therein and the view so held by the learned Additional
Magistrate of Kandy had been totally, endorsed by the Learned High Court Judge
of the Province while affirming the same.
However, the Appellants had sought to contend both in the High Court of the
Province in the application in revision as well as before this Court in appeal that
Rule 72 of the Co-operative Societies Rules has no application to the issue at
hand in view of the promulgation of the Co-operative Society Statute of the
Central Province of 10 of 1990-published in the Government Gazette bearing No.
75 and dated 22.01.1993, but not an iota of proof had been adduced in support
of the contention so advanced by the Appellant.
Page 16 of 17
Besides, upon a careful perusal of the Statute and the Co-operative Societies
Rules, they clearly, show that none of them contain anything which support the
contention so advanced by the Appellants.
Hence, I would hold that the petition filed in the High Court of the Province along
with the application in revision does not in any manner, disclose any exceptional
circumstances, warranting the exercise of this extra-ordinary method of
rectification by the High Court of the Province in revision of the MC order as
rightly, held by the High Court Judge of the Province.
I would therefore, see no illegality and/or impropriety and/or irregularity of the
MC order sought to be impugned by the Appellants in the application in revision
filed in the High Court of the Province nor of the HC order which affirmed the
MC order and which sought to be impugned by the Appellants in the instant
appeal before us.
In view of the foregoing, I would hold that instant appeal is not entitled to succeed
both in fact and law.
I would thus, hold that the contentions so advanced and the submissions so
made in support thereof, by the learned Counsel for the Appellants at the hearing
of this appeal before us by way of written submissions, are not entitled to
succeed in both fact and law and as such they should be rejected.
Hence, I would proceed to dismiss the instant appeal with costs.
As urged and agreed by the respective Counsel, I would hold that the Judgement
in the instant appeal will bind the parties in the appeals bearing Nos.
CA(PHC)0218/2018 and CA(PHC)0219/2018 too.
In the result, the appeals bearing Nos. CA(PHC)0218/2018 and
CA(PHC)0219/2018 too, would stand dismissed with costs.
Before I conclude, I would desire to state that it is still, open to the 2nd, 3rd and
4th Appellants to avail themselves of the defence that may be available to them
Page 17 of 17
under section 59(4) of the Statute as enumerated above, when this matter will
come up before the Magistrate Court of Kandy by way of further proceedings in
view of the decision of this Court if they are so advised.
I would direct the Registrar to cause a copy of the order of the instant appeal to
be filed of record in appeals bearing Nos CA(PHC)0218/2018 and
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
D. THOTAWATTA, J.
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
